title: Observable — How DNA Copies Itself
voice_suffix: clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only

shots.speech_text: Every time one of your cells divides, it copies three billion letters of DNA — and it almost never makes a mistake. How?

CONTINUITY LOCK @Set-Seamless-Neutral-001: identical neutral grey seamless — same backdrop tone mid-grey #9A9A98, same floor sweep, same subject-to-backdrop distance, same falloff — seamless continuation, zero jump cut. Presenter explains the phenomenon plainly, motivated gesture, clinical lighting. Camera: slow push-in medium. Neutral seamless 5500K bilateral soft key locked — symmetrical wrap, subtle nose shadow only, zero hue drift, no magenta vignette, no warm/cool lateral split. Neutral 5600–6500K even field — shadow definition for shape only, no gel, no warm practicals in frame. COLOR LOCK Seamless-Neutral: backdrop #9A9A98±2%, skin true-to-life, wardrobe colors accurate, no gel spill — no magenta edge gradient, no orange spotlight cone. Locked-off or slow linear track; no handheld. Lip-synced, delivers: "The double helix splits down the middle, and each old strand becomes a template for a new one — so every copy keeps one original strand. That's called semi-conservative. But the two strands run in opposite directions, so only one is copied smoothly; the other is built backwards in short pieces called Okazaki fragments, then stitched together." clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only. Finish on presentational gesture peak, never hold dead stillness or dead frame.
shots.speech_text: The double helix splits down the middle, and each old strand becomes a template for a new one — so every copy keeps one original strand. That's called semi-conservative. But the two strands run in opposite directions, so only one is copied smoothly; the other is built backwards in short pieces called Okazaki fragments, then stitched together.

shots.speech_text: We're not guessing. In nineteen fifty-eight, Meselson and Stahl tracked DNA by weight and proved the semi-conservative pattern. And the helix itself was pinned down atom by atom with X-ray crystallography — the B-DNA coordinates are public.
